audit_items.proof_trace: `nfp_ccm_mbox_mark_all_err` dequeues all batched skbs and sets state=DONE with error but never calls `dev_kfree_skb_any` or `dev_consume_skb_any` on posted skbs — unlike the success path `nfp_ccm_mbox_copy_out` (L341-347) which explicitly calls `dev_consume_skb_any(skb)` for every posted message. For non-posted skbs, waiting threads eventually call `nfp_ccm_mbox_skb_return` which frees on error; for posted skbs there is no waiter, so every posted skb dequeued by this function is permanently leaked. Both callers (`nfp_ccm_mbox_run_queue_unlock` L413 and `nfp_ccm_mbox_post_wait_work` L642) reach this path on firmware reconfig failure.
audit_items.attack_scenario: An attacker who can trigger repeated firmware mailbox errors (e.g., via malformed CMSG payloads or hardware fault injection) while driving posted-mode mailbox traffic causes unbounded kernel memory growth: each batch failure leaks all posted skbs allocated via `nfp_ccm_mbox_msg_alloc`, eventually exhausting kernel memory and crashing the system (OOM DoS).
